title: The Regina Monologues

jokes.text: Money is for the poor.
jokes.setup: Smithers suggests Burns carry his own money
jokes.punchline: Burns dismisses money as something only poor people need
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - only he could deliver this line with such absurd confidence. Excellent economy and ironic inversion of reality.

jokes.text: The automated teller machine-eola-trola-maton.
jokes.setup: Smithers suggests using an ATM card
jokes.punchline: Burns over-complicates the term with unnecessary syllables
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns antiquated language humor. Shows his disconnection from modern technology through verbal flourish.

jokes.text: Oh, Smithers, guide me in. / My pleasure, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns needs help using an ATM
jokes.punchline: Smithers' enthusiastic response to 'guide me in' has sexual undertones
jokes.explanation: Subtle innuendo playing on the Burns-Smithers dynamic. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Smithers, what's my password? / It's your age, sir. / Excellent.
jokes.setup: Burns asks for his ATM password
jokes.punchline: His password is his extremely advanced age, which he finds excellent
jokes.explanation: Efficient multi-layer joke: Burns is so old his age makes a secure password, and he's pleased about it.

jokes.text: What's the smallest amount of money I can think of? A thousand dollars!
jokes.setup: Burns thinks about withdrawing a small amount
jokes.punchline: $1000 is his idea of 'small'
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns wealth disconnect. The setup-punchline structure works well with his character.

jokes.text: Oh! My moolah! It's escaping my clutches!
jokes.setup: Money comes out of the ATM
jokes.punchline: Burns dramatically personifies money as trying to escape
jokes.explanation: Fun character moment with Burns' theatrical language, though not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: Feel the drunken wrath of Chuck Shadowski!
jokes.setup: Hockey dad fighting game continues
jokes.punchline: Specific character name with 'drunken wrath'
jokes.explanation: Decent character name and the 'drunken wrath' phrasing is amusing.

jokes.text: Dad, stop! It's only assault. Don't make it murder!
jokes.setup: Child trying to calm violent father
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions 'only assault' as if that's acceptable
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's well-crafted. The casual tone about assault vs murder is genuinely funny and subversive.

jokes.text: You're a good friend, Bart. / The best you'll ever have.
jokes.setup: Homer removes ladybug from Bart's hair
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately declares himself Bart's best friend ever
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer ego - helps minimally then declares himself the best friend Bart will ever have.

jokes.text: Do you know how much furniture we could rent with this?
jokes.setup: Finding $1000
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks of renting (not buying) furniture
jokes.explanation: Shows the Simpson family's financial situation through Homer's immediate thought to rent furniture.

jokes.text: Marge, this is why people don't tell you things.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests returning found money
jokes.punchline: Homer and Bart's simultaneous reaction about not telling Marge things
jokes.explanation: Great family dynamic moment. The simultaneous realization is perfectly timed and very quotable.

jokes.text: Well, we put up a flyer. Let's see someone claim that money now.
jokes.setup: Expected to put up a proper 'found money' flyer
jokes.punchline: Presumably put up an illegible or unhelpful flyer
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup - Bart maliciously complies with Marge's request but sabotages it.

jokes.text: You know the drill. Try to land on my back-fat.
jokes.setup: Someone is about to fall
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy offers his back-fat as a landing cushion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy line - self-aware about his physical state while being helpful.

jokes.text: Whose picture's on it? / It would have to be somebody famous. I'm gonna say, Hitler.
jokes.setup: Bart asks claimant to describe the bill
jokes.punchline: Person guesses Hitler is on US currency
jokes.explanation: Absurd guess that reveals the person has no idea what's on money, but is trying to fake it.

jokes.text: Does the bill have bank teller blood on it? / No, it doesn't. / I'm sorry I wasted your time.
jokes.setup: Bart continues screening claimants
jokes.punchline: Person admits the bill doesn't have blood on it and apologizes
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with good absurdist escalation. The polite apology makes it funnier.

jokes.text: No one's touching the hors d'oeuvres.
jokes.setup: Elaborate party setup
jokes.punchline: Classic party host concern about untouched fancy food
jokes.explanation: Standard party host anxiety. Bart trying to be fancy is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: Yeah. But you always wind up kidnapped or on a chain gang.
jokes.setup: Homer claims they've had great vacations
jokes.punchline: Marge lists their vacation disasters
jokes.explanation: Good callback to series history. The escalation from kidnapped to chain gang works well.

jokes.text: I still don't know why you had that photo enlarged.
jokes.setup: Marge mentions not wanting to discuss their honeymoon
jokes.punchline: Homer references an enlarged photo from their honeymoon
jokes.explanation: Implies something embarrassing happened that Homer memorialized. Good character-specific humor.

jokes.text: Look at the cross-hatching on Grover Cleveland's tie. I completely buy the illusion of shadow.
jokes.setup: Kids looking at the thousand dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's pretentious art criticism of currency design
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse - nerdy kid applying art criticism to money. The specific terms make it funnier.

jokes.text: Time's up. Beat it, do-rags.
jokes.setup: Kids examining the bill
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy calls them 'do-rags' instead of a normal dismissive term
jokes.explanation: Comic Book Guy's weird insult choice fits his character. Mildly amusing wordplay.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm charging five bucks to get in. They deserve to see the boy behind the bill.
jokes.setup: Bart has created a museum around the bill
jokes.punchline: He includes himself as an exhibit - 'the boy behind the bill'
jokes.explanation: Good entrepreneurial Bart moment. The phrasing 'boy behind the bill' is nicely crafted.

jokes.text: You can't whitewash history, Lise.
jokes.setup: Lisa criticizes Bart's exhibit as biased
jokes.punchline: Bart uses academic language to defend his clearly biased exhibit
jokes.explanation: Bart using sophisticated rhetoric while being completely wrong is classic character humor.

jokes.text: A subscription to BARTFORUM Magazine, and Bart will say 'Eat my shorts' on your home answering machine.
jokes.setup: Someone asks about museum membership benefits
jokes.punchline: Parody of ARTFORUM with personalized catchphrase service
jokes.explanation: Clever ARTFORUM parody combined with Bart's catchphrase as a service. Good integration.

jokes.text: That would be hilarious. That's just me, though. You know, I love to laugh.
jokes.setup: Person asks about crank calls
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly over-explains their sense of humor
jokes.explanation: Decent awkward character moment but not particularly memorable or well-crafted.

jokes.text: Prove it, Bony Curtis.
jokes.setup: Old man claims the bill belongs to him
jokes.punchline: Bart calls him 'Bony Curtis' (play on Tony Curtis)
jokes.explanation: Decent pun playing on the man's thinness, though not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Had my heart been inside at the time, it could've been fatal.
jokes.setup: Man explains bruise from the bill hitting his chest
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions his heart not being inside his body
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist line delivered matter-of-factly. The casualness of mentioning his heart being external is perfectly surreal.

jokes.text: The Hard Rock Cafe in Phoenix has better crap.
jokes.setup: Museum loses its main attraction
jokes.punchline: Specific comparison to Hard Rock Cafe Phoenix's 'crap'
jokes.explanation: Good specific reference. Phoenix Hard Rock Cafe is oddly specific which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Finally, I can get what I've always wanted. A used Toyota.
jokes.setup: Bart has $3000 from his museum
jokes.punchline: His dream purchase is a used Toyota
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - 10-year-old Bart's modest dream car is endearing and realistic.

jokes.text: I'd like to return to Brazil, but I hear the monkey problem is even worse now.
jokes.setup: Family discussing vacation destinations
jokes.punchline: Marge casually references a 'monkey problem' in Brazil
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd throwaway line. 'Monkey problem' treated as a serious tourist concern.

jokes.text: I say. Ohhh! Nice.
jokes.setup: Grampa's wartime romance flashback
jokes.punchline: British woman's prim commentary during intimate moment
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing contrast between British propriety and the situation, though not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: You have my word as a gentleman and a lady.
jokes.setup: Homer promises to behave on the trip
jokes.punchline: Calls himself both a gentleman and a lady
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer malapropism. Shows his attempt to sound formal while being confused.

jokes.text: Wow! I can't believe we met Mr. Bean!
jokes.setup: Family just met Tony Blair
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks they met Mr. Bean instead
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer cultural confusion. Functional but predictable.

jokes.text: Every cab has its own butler.
jokes.setup: Homer observes London cab service
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets shared cab situation as butler service
jokes.explanation: Good Homer misunderstanding of British culture and class system.

jokes.text: We saved your ass in Vietnam and shared our prostitutes with Hugh Grant!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to impress as American tourist
jokes.punchline: Historically inaccurate claim plus Hugh Grant prostitute reference
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - wrong about history but specific pop culture reference. Good character integration.

jokes.text: I wouldn't dream of it, sir.
jokes.setup: Homer demands no dry British wit
jokes.punchline: Clerk delivers perfectly dry British wit in response
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and character work. Exactly what was requested not to happen.

jokes.text: Sorry I bothered you.
jokes.setup: Grampa's increasingly specific questions about 1944 soldier
jokes.punchline: Polite apology after getting too personal with stranger
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Grampa's social awkwardness, but not particularly strong.

jokes.text: Can you tell me what happens at the end of the series? / He grows up and marries you. Is that what you want to hear? / Yes.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks about Harry Potter ending
jokes.punchline: Rowling gives dismissive fake answer, Lisa accepts it happily
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity cameo joke. Rowling's exasperation and Lisa's satisfaction work well together.

jokes.text: Now completely free of 'Mad Fish Disease.'
jokes.setup: British fish and chips restaurant
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'Mad Cow Disease' as 'Mad Fish Disease'
jokes.explanation: Decent pun on the Mad Cow Disease scare. Topical and reasonably clever.

jokes.text: Fish? I don't know. I'm not really a vegetarian.
jokes.setup: Offered fish at restaurant
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused logic about fish and vegetarianism
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer dietary confusion. Shows his odd thought processes.

jokes.text: That was over soccer results. Can you believe they gave Giggs a yellow card in the box?
jokes.setup: Marge points out Homer punched three people
jokes.punchline: Homer defends it with specific soccer terminology he doesn't understand
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - using sports jargon he doesn't understand to justify violence.

jokes.text: I understand the word 'gave,' unless it means something else in this country!
jokes.setup: Marge asks if Homer understands what he just said
jokes.punchline: Homer's defensive response about understanding one word
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer defensiveness and paranoia about cultural differences. Well-crafted escalation.

jokes.text: Look, teabag, just fork over the brown.
jokes.setup: Candy shop warning about British sweets
jokes.punchline: Homer's crude British slang attempt asking for candy
jokes.explanation: Homer's attempt at British slang is amusingly crude and wrong.

jokes.text: There's Jimmy Page, one of the greatest thieves of American black music who ever walked the earth.
jokes.setup: Homer pointing out London sights
jokes.punchline: Calls Jimmy Page a thief of black music among tourist attractions
jokes.explanation: Sharp musical criticism delivered casually as tourist commentary. Surprisingly sophisticated for Homer.

jokes.text: You can't bet on my kids! This is America, pal!
jokes.setup: British lords want to bet on civilizing the kids
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks he's in America while clearly in England
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer geographical confusion, though fairly standard for the character.

jokes.text: Sir Ian McKellen! You're my favorite Shakespearean actor.
jokes.setup: Meeting famous actor
jokes.punchline: Lisa's earnest fangirl moment with specific actor appreciation
jokes.explanation: Standard Lisa intellectual appreciation moment. In-character but not particularly funny.

jokes.text: You mean Macbeth? / Quiet, you blundering fool! You'll curse us all!
jokes.setup: McKellen warns about not naming 'this particular play'
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately says Macbeth, causing theatrical horror
jokes.explanation: Good setup-payoff with theater superstition. Bart's obliviousness drives the comedy well.

jokes.text: Macbeth! Macbeth! Macbeth!
jokes.setup: Already said Macbeth once by accident
jokes.punchline: Bart gleefully repeats it multiple times
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - once he knows something's taboo, he doubles down. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Mom, you said Macbeth!
jokes.setup: Marge tries to stop Bart from saying Macbeth
jokes.punchline: Bart points out she said it too
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the gag. Bart's logic is sound which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It's bad luck to say that, too!
jokes.setup: Homer wishes McKellen 'Good luck'
jokes.punchline: Even 'good luck' is bad luck in theater
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of theater superstitions. Efficient punchline that extends the joke logically.

jokes.text: Yes, this trip is entirely uneventful.
jokes.setup: Family commenting on peaceful trip
jokes.punchline: Homer's comment immediately precedes chaos
jokes.explanation: Classic setup for disaster. Telegraphed but effective dramatic irony.

jokes.text: Oh, that thing is supposed to be on fire.
jokes.setup: Homer surveying car crash damage
jokes.punchline: Claims something obviously broken was meant to be that way
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer denial and rationalization. The confidence in his absurd claim is funny.

jokes.text: Let the guys handle this.
jokes.setup: Woman tries to help with crash aftermath
jokes.punchline: Homer's sexist assumption while being completely incompetent himself
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer sexism joke. In-character but not particularly clever or fresh.

jokes.text: Your Majesty? (mimics whipping)
jokes.setup: Told he rear-ended the Queen
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissive whip-crack gesture
jokes.explanation: Homer's disrespectful gesture fits his character but isn't particularly inventive.

jokes.text: Which means her real name must be... Henrietta R. Hippo!
jokes.setup: Homer sees 'H.R.H.' on Queen's luggage
jokes.punchline: Deduces it stands for 'Henrietta R. Hippo'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic leap. The specificity of 'Henrietta R. Hippo' makes it funnier than a generic name.

jokes.text: What difference could it make? He hit the friggin' Queen!
jokes.setup: Asked why she let Homer represent himself
jokes.punchline: Marge's exasperated logic about the hopeless situation
jokes.explanation: Good Marge exasperation moment. Her logic is sound which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I'm a man! / And a very manly one if I may say so, m'Lord.
jokes.setup: Homer calls the male judge a grandmother
jokes.punchline: Homer's awkward attempt to recover from the mistake
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer awkwardness. The over-compensation is mildly amusing but predictable.

jokes.text: If she were 100 years younger and I were 100 years older... Oh!
jokes.setup: Homer admiring the Queen's courtroom performance
jokes.punchline: Starts inappropriate comment, realizes the math doesn't work
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment where his crude comment is derailed by his own mathematical realization.

jokes.text: America rules! Our Beatles are way better than your precious Rolling Stones!
jokes.setup: Homer making patriotic outburst
jokes.punchline: Claims American Beatles are better than British Rolling Stones
jokes.explanation: Homer's confused nationalism and mixed-up band origins. Shows his cultural confusion well.

jokes.text: Poke the monster with a stick! Tuppence a jab!
jokes.setup: Bart treating imprisoned Homer as tourist attraction
jokes.punchline: Charging to poke Homer with authentic British pricing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart entrepreneurship. The authentic British pricing ('tuppence') shows good attention to detail.

jokes.text: In return, I will spell the word 'color' with a 'U.'
jokes.setup: Homer praying to 'God of England'
jokes.punchline: Offers to adopt British spelling as sacrifice
jokes.explanation: Good cultural difference joke. Homer treating spelling as a religious sacrifice is amusing.

jokes.text: Oh, I can't do it! It's so stupid!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to use metric system in prayer
jokes.punchline: Breaks character to declare metric system stupid
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer - his American stubbornness overrides even desperate prayer. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Don't buy any videotapes in England! They won't work in our VCR!
jokes.setup: Homer's final words to Marge before potential execution
jokes.punchline: Completely mundane technical advice instead of romantic sentiment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - technical incompatibility over love declaration. Subverts romantic death scene brilliantly.

jokes.text: Don't worry it. Let the pike do the work.
jokes.setup: Guards practicing beheading with melons
jokes.punchline: Casual execution technique advice
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with craftsman-like casualness. The specific technique advice makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Goodbye, sparkly elves! / Goodbye!
jokes.setup: Homer escaping through burning tunnel
jokes.punchline: Friendly farewell to his fire-induced hallucinations
jokes.explanation: Charming absurdist moment. The politeness of the farewell to hallucinations is endearing.

jokes.text: Boy, a Krispy Kreme would really clean up down here.
jokes.setup: Homer in elephant enclosure/sewers
jokes.punchline: Business opportunity observation in gross situation
jokes.explanation: Good Homer observation. The brand specificity and business mindset in a crisis fits his character.

jokes.text: who by the way has never had a girlfriend. I'm just saying.
jokes.setup: Homer comparing America to 'goody two-shoes brother Canada'
jokes.punchline: Casual aside about Canada never having a girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Brilliant anthropomorphization of countries as family members. The casual cruelty of the aside is perfect.

jokes.text: English women don't pump gas naked. / See you in Atlanta, bitch!
jokes.setup: Madonna claiming to be English
jokes.punchline: Homer's crude dismissal reveals her true American nature
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity satire. Madonna's instant reversion to American behavior when challenged works well.

jokes.text: Plus, as the boat pulled away from the dock, I thought you looked fat.
jokes.setup: Grampa apologizing for leaving Edwina
jokes.punchline: Admits shallow reason for abandoning wartime romance
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest and character-perfect. The casual cruelty after the romantic buildup is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Well, gotta go! See you in heaven!
jokes.setup: Grampa realizes Abbey might be his daughter
jokes.punchline: Awkward exit with dark implication about their ages
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa exit. The casual assumption about death timeline is darkly funny.

jokes.text: You make Dame Edna look like a dude.
jokes.setup: Homer complimenting Abbey
jokes.punchline: Compares her to Dame Edna (a drag queen character)
jokes.explanation: Homer's attempt at compliment that's accidentally accurate since Dame Edna is actually a man in drag.

tmdb_overview: After making over $3000 from his own museum featuring a $1000 bill, Bart takes the family to London (on the suggestion of Grampa), where Homer is arrested for treason after crashing into the Queen.
